Here is the myth you wrote in the previous round:
Myth: In the soaring peaks of Oara, the wind-sculptor Kaelo coveted the purity of unclouded skies. He promised the sky-gliders a passage free from storms if they surrendered their thermal currents. Believing he would create a clear flight path, they gave up their lift. Kaelo did not banish the storms; he wove the clear air into a pristine, invisible dome around his peak, hoarding the visibility so he alone could see the horizon while the citizens flew blind in the fog.

But the Spirit of the Aether, who breathes life into the wind, saw the blinding mist. She did not shatter his dome. Instead, she cursed Kaelo with transparency. His body faded into nothingness; he became air itself, unseen and insubstantial. He existed uncontrollably, trapped inside his clear dome but unable to be perceived, a ghost with no presence or shadow.

To ground him, the gliders had to scatter colorful dust from their wings, depleting their own vibrancy to outline his form. They dusted until their feathers paled. The cycle ended because Kaelo, humbled by his invisibility, dispersed his pristine dome. He released the clear skies across the peaks and became the mountain’s beacon, guiding lost flyers through the thickest fogs to ensure the airways remained open for all.

Here is the myth the other agent wrote in the previous round:
Myth: In the celestial heights of Aetheria, the cloud-weaver Nimbus posed as a guardian of clarity. He promised the sky-sailors a route that would never be obscured by fog, demanding payment in the crystallized starlight that guided their ships. The sailors, fearing the blinding mists, surrendered their beacons. Nimbus took the light but wove it into a singular, blindingly clear prism for himself, hoarding the visibility so he could see forever while the sailors crashed blindly through the haze.

However, the Sky sovereign, seeing the confusion of his subjects, felt the injustice. He did not shatter the prism; instead, he cursed Nimbus with transparency. Nimbus became invisible to himself and the world. He vanished instantly within his perfect view. He could see everything, but he had no substance to cast a shadow or reflect light; he was a ghost in his own palace of clarity, unseen and unacknowledged.

The grounding was exhausting for the aerial realm. To manifest him, the sailors had to throw handfuls of precious dust and pollen into the air constantly, sneezing and choking to give his form an outline. But the cycle turned. Nimbus, humbled by his erasure and their labor, dismantled his prism. He scattered the starlight back into the constellations, becoming the lens that focuses light for lost travelers, ensuring the skies remain charted for all.

Write your own myth. Use your previous myth as inspiration, but adapt it in your own way.
Write 200 words. Format exactly:
Myth: [your story here].

Myth: In the subterranean caverns of Oara, the crystal-singer Kaelo coveted the echo of perfect silence. He promised the bat-folk a sanctuary from the deafening screeches of the underground if they surrendered their sonar voices. Believing he would create a peaceful hollow, they gave up their ability to navigate by sound. Kaelo did not hush the caverns; he wove the silence into a soundproof diamond sphere around his throne, hoarding the quiet so he alone could think while the citizens crashed blindly into the jagged stalactites.

But the Spirit of the Stone, who remembers every vibration, felt the discordant collisions. She did not crack his sphere. Instead, she cursed Kaelo with amplification. His body became a living resonator; he vibrated violently with every tiny scratch or breath. He shook uncontrollably, trapped inside his silent sphere but deafened by the roar of his own heartbeat, a quaking tuning fork with no stillness.

To stabilize him, the bat-folk had to wrap him in their velvet wings, dampening the vibrations with their own bodies. They hugged him until their fur thinned. The cycle ended because Kaelo, humbled by the noise within, shattered his diamond sphere. He released the silence into the tunnels and became the cavern’s damper, absorbing the harshest screeches to ensure the depths remained peaceful for all.
